frameworks.name: perceptual discrimination as ground truth for similarity
frameworks.one_line: Subjective similarity tracks an observer's own near-threshold discrimination capacity.

frameworks.name: ill-posed similarity problem
frameworks.one_line: Prior view that similarity lacks an objective basis is challenged.

frameworks.name: signal-detection / near-threshold psychophysics
frameworks.one_line: Discrimination capacity is operationalized in near-threshold settings.

core_question: Do subjective perceptual similarity judgments reflect an individual's own perceptual discrimination capacities?
key_assumptions: Discrimination capacity can serve as a quasi-objective ground truth for similarity
Similarity reflects feature dimensions on which the observer is themselves limited
Near-threshold discrimination is the relevant operating regime
manipulations: Pairs of face stimuli used in similarity ranking
Same pairs probed in a separate discrimination task
key_findings: Positive association between discrimination capacity and subjective dissimilarity
Association is specific to each individual observer
interpretations: Perceptual similarity judgments reflect and predict one's own discrimination capacities
An individual's discrimination capacity provides a quasi-objective basis for similarity
